title: -Restaurant Inspections Audit Report Wins National Recognition

sections.current_situation:
Each year, local government audit organizations from around the U.S. and Canada submit their
best performance audit reports for judging. The judges from peer organizations determined that
our audit of the City of Berkeley’s food inspection program was among the best of 2024.
sections.background:
We issued our audit report, Berkeley Restaurant Inspections: Chronically Understaffed Program
Did Not Meet Targets, on July 10, 2024.1
The purpose of the audit was to determine:
1. To what extent is the Food Safety and Inspection Program meeting its inspection
targets?
2. To what extent is the Food Safety and Inspection Program adequately funded and
staffed?
3. How well does the Food Safety and Inspection Program keep the public informed about
food safety?
We found that the Environmental Health Division (Division) did not meet its targets for food
facility inspections in 2023, increasing health risk. Almost half (45 percent) of Berkeley’s food
facilities were not inspected, including 193 restaurants. Further, the Division responded to only
26 percent of potential foodborne illness complaints within the target of one business day.
1 https://berkeleyca.gov/sites/default/files/documents/Berkeley_Restaurant_Inspections_-
_Chronically_Understaffed_Program_Did_Not_Meet_Targets.pdf
2180 Milvia Street, Berkeley, CA 94704 ● Tel: (510) 981-6750 ● TDD: (510) 981-6903
E-mail: auditor@berkeleyca.gov Website: http://www.berkeleyca.gov/your-government/city-audits
Page 2 of 4
Internal
INFORMATION CALENDAR
Additionally, the Division did not separately track food inspection revenue and spending from its
other inspection programs over the audit period. Short staffing increased inspectors’ workloads
and made it difficult to meet targets and monitor program performance. Public information on
food inspections was limited and hard to access. Berkeley provided less information about
inspections online compared to other jurisdictions. Berkeley did not use placards, a common
practice for communicating food safety used by other jurisdictions including Alameda County.
We made nine recommendations to improve inspection coverage and quickly respond to
alleged foodborne illness complaints, as well as increase transparency and accessibility. City
Management agreed or partially agreed to our findin
sections.environmental:
There are no identifiable environmental effects or opportunities associated with this report.
POSSIBLE FUTURE ACTION
We will continue to follow up on the status of audit recommendation implementation to ensure
the City mitigates the risks that could prevent the City from providing efficient, effective, and
equitable service delivery.
sections.fiscal_impacts:
Audit work leads to new or enhanced revenue, cost recovery, and increased efficiency, with
economic impact well beyond the audit costs. Implementing the audit recommendations from
the restaurant inspections audit will ensure that the Environmental Health Division improves
coverage of food inspections and can provide the public with complete and accessible
information on food safety, minimizing public health risk to the community.
Staffing up the food inspection program may carry financial implications depending on the
specific implementation strategies. However, filling budgeted positions is necessary to meet
inspection targets. Additionally, updating the information shared online and implementing
placarding may require initial investments in design and implementation. We anticipate the
ongoing costs of placarding to be minimal, as placards are updated as part of the routine
inspection process.
CONTACT PERSON
Jenny Wong, City Auditor, City Auditor’s Office, 510-981-6750

1: 2024 Knighton Award Letter from ALGA
Pg. 2 of 2
Page 3 of 4
Association of Local Government Auditors
April 4, 2025
Jenny Wong
2180 Milvia St. Third Floor
Berkeley, California 94704
RE: 2024 Knighton Award Winner
Dear Jenny,
The Association of Local Government Auditors’ Awards Program Committee is pleased to
announce that the City of Berkeley, City Auditor's Office’s report, “Berkeley Restaurant
Inspections: Chronically Understaffed Program Did Not Meet Targets,” won the 2024
Distinguished Knighton Award in the medium shop category.
ALGA was founded in 1985 to serve auditors who specialize in working with local governments,
and our members represent over 300 local government audit organizations across the United
States and Canada. As you well know, local government auditors are committed to helping their
governments provide services to the public more efficiently and effectively with the limited
resources available to them.
To encourage increasing levels of excellence among local government auditors, ALGA created
the Knighton Award in 1995 to recognize the best performance audit reports produced by local
government audit shops in the previous year. Each year, local government audit organizations,
like yours, submit their best performance audit report to be evaluated against four criteria:
(1) An audit scope that has the potential for significant impact and is responsive to the
needs and concerns of decision-makers and/or the public.
(2) Audit conclusions that are persuasive, logical, and firmly supported by the evidence,
which was gathered using appropriate research methods and tools.
(3) Audit recommendations that are feasible and will make government programs more
effective and efficient.
(4) Audit results that are communicated clearly and concisely.
Judges from peer organizations determined your office’s report was among the best of 2024.
The judges commented:
The report is well written, easy to follow, and digs all the way down to root causes. The

sections.preamble:
Page 1 of 4
INFORMATION CALENDAR
April 29, 2025
To: Honorable Mayor and Members of the City Council
From: Jenny Wong, City Auditor
Subject: Restaurant Inspections Audit Report Wins National Recognition
INTRODUCTION
The City of Berkeley Auditor’s Office has won the 2024 Distinguished Knighton Award for one of
the best performance audit reports in the medium shop category from the Association of Local
Government Auditors (ALGA). The report, Berkeley Restaurant Inspections: Chronically
Understaffed Program Did Not Meet Targets, was judged on several key elements, such as the
potential for significant impact, the persuasiveness of conclusions, the focus on improving
government efficiency and effectiveness, and its clarity and conciseness.
